title: E5.3: PearBee — Hyperbee KV wrapper end-to-end
description:
CONTEXT: E5 wrapper 2 of 5. Hyperbee (KV) on the Hypercore substrate. Same hand-written pattern as E5.2. Range streams are the first real consumer of the schema's event-stream subscribe/unsubscribe primitive — implement subscription lifecycle properly (unsubscribe on Dart stream cancel; no leaked JS listeners).
FILES: pear-end (+hyperbee dep, license-check + regenerate THIRD_PARTY_LICENSES), packages/flutter_pear/lib/src/bee.dart (PearBee), schema additions, fake extension, tests.
API SHAPE: get/put/del, range read as Stream (bounded snapshot semantics documented), watch as broadcast Stream. Uint8List values, PearKey-friendly keys, dispose().
VALIDATION: fake tests: put/get/del, range over N entries in order, watch fires on change, cancel unsubscribes (assert via fake hook that the JS-side listener count drops); error paths (get on closed bee). ONE real-device test: put on A, watch fires on B over replication.

BEFORE CLOSING — VALIDATION + REVIEW (mandatory, in order):
1. Run: melos run analyze — zero new warnings.
2. Run the task-specific tests named in VALIDATION above — all green. New logic MUST have its test in the same diff (no follow-up-test promises).
3. Self-review the diff line-by-line against ACCEPTANCE below; every criterion demonstrably met, none "mostly".
4. Check the parent epic description: confirm no LOCKED decision was reversed or watered down.
5. Every new/changed public symbol has dartdoc.
6. Non-trivial diff (>50 lines or touches rpc/native/pear-end): run /code-review (or a second-model review) and fix P1 findings before close.
7. Post a closing comment: what changed, test evidence (command + result), anything discovered worth a new bead.
acceptance_criteria: get/put/del/range/watch per API shape; stream cancel provably unsubscribes JS-side; fake tests cover happy+error+ordering; real-device watch-over-replication test passes; licenses regenerated.
notes:
IMPLEMENTATION: PearBee (packages/flutter_pear/lib/src/bee.dart, new) -- a single class (not a two-class PearStore/PearCore-style split, matching project_plan.md section 4's table listing "Hyperbee | PearBee" as one class) with a static PearBee.open(rpc, {name, key}) factory mirroring PearSwarm.join's precedent. get/put/del as Futures, range() as a bounded-snapshot Stream (whole result fetched in one request/response then emitted via an async* generator -- documented as NOT live, matching the locked no-chunked-streaming-yet decision), watch() as a broadcast Stream using the swarm.join/leave subscribe-lifecycle pattern (bee.watch/bee.unwatch RPC methods + a bee.update event tagged with a caller-generated watchId, since a bee can have >1 concurrent watch). replicate() added beyond the ticket's shorthand API-shape line -- the ticket's own real-device VALIDATION ("put on A, watch fires on B over replication") is only achievable if the underlying core actually replicates, so this mirrors PearCore.replicate exactly, including reusing E5.2's shared replicationStreams chaining so a future core+bee pair can multiplex one connection safely.

pear-end/index.js: hyperbee ^2.27.3 added (license-checked: all 90 resolved node_modules packages MIT/Apache-2.0/BSD/ISC, matching project convention); bees/closedBees/beeWatchers registries; BEE_WATCH consumes a Hyperbee Watcher's async-iterator in a detached loop, forwarding each change as a tagged bee.update event. Applied E5.2's review lessons proactively this time: exactly-one-of-name/key validation, correct reopen-after-close guard (!registry.has || closedSet.has, not just the former), withStorageErrors wrapping every handler. Schema: 9 new PearMethod entries, 1 event, 2 error codes (unknownBee/beeClosed), all mirrored in schema.js and cross-checked in schema_test.dart. flutter_pear_test's FakeBareWorklet extended: _FakeBee (ordered SplayTreeMap keyed by hex-encoded raw bytes -- verified equivalent to byte-lexicographic comparison including differing-length keys), writer-only put/del enforcement, per-worklet name salting, and the same two-sided replicate handshake as _FakeCore, plus a KV-analogue safety check (subset-with-matching-values instead of prefix) before any merge.

TESTS: packages/flutter_pear/test/bee_test.dart (new, 21 tests): put/get/del happy path, range ordering + gte/lt bounds + reverse/limit (this ticket's VALIDATION named all three), watch-fires-on-change, cancel-unsubscribes-worklet-side (asserted via a new FakeBareWorklet.activeBeeWatchCount introspection hook -- the ticket's VALIDATION explicitly asked for this), BEE_CLOSED error path (get AND put on a closed bee), reopen-after-close, per-worklet name-key non-collision, writer-only-put enforcement, one-sided-replicate-never-syncs, and a full two-peer replicate flow (A puts before replicating, B attaches by key, both replicate, B reads A's data and sees a live watch update for a post-replicate put). schema_test.dart extended for the 9 new methods/1 event/2 error codes. melos run analyze: 0 warnings across all 4 packages. melos run test --no-select: 84/84 in flutter_pear (was 63 before E5.2+E5.3 this session), 13/13 flutter_pear_test, 6/6 flutter_pear_bare -- all green. dart run flutter_pear:pack rebuilt the real bundle successfully with hyperbee's dependencies linked (87 modules in THIRD_PARTY_LICENSES, up from 80 after E5.2's corestore alone).

REVIEW: ran the review through the Workflow tool (3 parallel angles -- JS/Dart correctness, fake-worklet conformance, schema/conventions -- each verified via an independent adversarial pass as its own review completed, per this ticket's BEFORE CLOSING step 6). schema-conventions came back clean (0 findings). The other two surfaced 3 real, CONFIRMED bugs, all fixed:
- CRITICAL (js-dart-correctness): watch()'s watchId was generated ONCE per watch() call, not per listen cycle. A cancel-then-immediately-relisten on the SAME Stream (e.g. a rebuilt widget) fires onCancel then onListen in that order, but a broadcast StreamController's onCancel is NOT awaited by subscription.cancel() (confirmed empirically: cancel()'s returned Future resolves before onCancel's async body finishes) -- so the stale onCancel's bee.unwatch call was still in flight when the fresh onListen reused the identical watchId, and pear-end's BEE_WATCH unconditionally overwrote its own registry entry with no close -- the OLD watcher leaked forever and the stale unwatch, arriving after, killed the FRESH watch instead. Fixed: watchId is now generated fresh inside onListen every cycle, captured into a local before onCancel's first await (avoiding a second race where a later onListen could reassign the outer variable before the paused onCancel resumes). New regression test added.
- MEDIUM (js-dart-correctness): BEE_UNWATCH resolved purely by watchId with no check that the entry's bee matches the caller's p.bee, unlike BEE_CLOSE's cleanup loop which does scope by bee -- a mismatched pair would silently close the wrong bee's watch with no error. Fixed (both real index.js and the fake, for conformance): throws UNKNOWN_BEE on a bee/watchId mismatch, matching the already-scoped BEE_CLOSE. New regression test added (drives the raw RPC methods directly, since PearBee's own public API never constructs a mismatched pair).
- HIGH (fake-conformance): _FakeBeeWatch.bee was final and never repointed during a replicate merge -- Set membership moved to the canonical shared bee correctly, but the watch's OWN .bee reference still pointed at the orphaned pre-merge object, so a later beeUnwatch/beeClose was a no-op on dead state, permanently leaking the watch (spurious future events, an uncollectable worklet reference) while FakeBareWorklet.activeBeeWatchCount reported 0 and masked it. Fixed: .bee is now mutable and repointed to canonical during the merge. New regression test added (extends the existing two-peer replicate test: cancel B's post-merge watch and confirm activeBeeWatchCount actually drops to 0).
Re-ran melos analyze + melos run test --no-select after every fix -- green throughout, final counts above.

DEFERRED (per this project's standing "automated tests first, hardware last" decision -- same treatment as E1.5/E5.1/E5.2): this ticket's VALIDATION also calls for "ONE real-device test: put on A, watch fires on B over replication." That leg is physical two-device hardware, deferred and tracked centrally in flutter_pear-doi -- not silently dropped. Everything else in ACCEPTANCE (get/put/del/range/watch per API shape, stream-cancel provably unsubscribes JS-side, fake tests cover happy+error+ordering, licenses regenerated) is fully met now.
close_reason: E5.3 done: PearBee (Hyperbee KV wrapper 2 of 5) implemented end-to-end -- get/put/del, bounded range() Stream, live watch() broadcast Stream with proper subscribe/unsubscribe lifecycle, and replicate() (added beyond the ticket's API-shape shorthand since the real-device VALIDATION scenario needs it). 21 new tests (84/84 green in flutter_pear) covering every VALIDATION item: range ordering + bounds, watch-fires-on-change, cancel-unsubscribes-worklet-side (new activeBeeWatchCount introspection hook), and BEE_CLOSED/error paths. Workflow-orchestrated review (3 angles, adversarial verify) found and fixed 1 critical + 1 high + 1 medium real bug: a watchId-reuse race that could kill a fresh watch and leak the stale one on rapid cancel-then-relisten, a missing bee-ownership check on unwatch, and a fake-conformance gap where a replicated watch's bee reference never repointed to the canonical object post-merge (silent leak). All three got regression tests. Real bundle rebuilt via dart run flutter_pear:pack with hyperbee linked for Android (87 modules, up from 80). Deps license-checked (MIT/Apache/BSD/ISC only). The two-device physical-replicate leg of VALIDATION is deferred with E1.5/E5.1/E5.2 under flutter_pear-doi, per this project's automated-first/hardware-last decision.

title: E5.2: PearStore/PearCore — Corestore+Hypercore wrapper end-to-end
description:
CONTEXT: E5 wrapper 1 of 5 — the SUBSTRATE (locked order: Corestore/Hypercore first; Hyperbee/Hyperdrive/Autobase sit on them). Hand-write against the schema (locked: NO codegen until after wrapper 3's assessment). Pattern for every wrapper: expose JS module over RPC -> typed Dart wrapper -> extend fake -> tests (fake + real device). Never reimplement protocol logic in Dart.
FILES: pear-end/index.js(+corestore/hypercore deps — license-check MIT/Apache/BSD/ISC, reject copyleft, regenerate THIRD_PARTY_LICENSES via pack), new packages/flutter_pear/lib/src/store.dart (PearStore, PearCore), schema additions, flutter_pear_test fake extension, tests.
API SHAPE (project_plan section 4): PearStore (open/get core by key), PearCore: append, get(i), length, replicate over a PearConnection, watch/update events. Futures for calls, broadcast Streams for events, Uint8List payloads, PearKey keys, dispose(), dartdoc on every symbol. Storage path per-platform via worklet app-storage.
ERRORS: register storage err.codes -> PearStorageException routing; catalog entries (problem+cause+fix+anchor) added when E8 catalog exists — leave codes registered + TODO markers otherwise.
VALIDATION: unit tests on fake (append/get/length/watch happy + error paths incl. get out-of-range, closed-core write); ONE real-device integration test: append on device A, replicate over swarm, read on device B (guards fake/real drift — codex #9).

BEFORE CLOSING — VALIDATION + REVIEW (mandatory, in order):
1. Run: melos run analyze — zero new warnings.
2. Run the task-specific tests named in VALIDATION above — all green. New logic MUST have its test in the same diff (no follow-up-test promises).
3. Self-review the diff line-by-line against ACCEPTANCE below; every criterion demonstrably met, none "mostly".
4. Check the parent epic description: confirm no LOCKED decision was reversed or watered down.
5. Every new/changed public symbol has dartdoc.
6. Non-trivial diff (>50 lines or touches rpc/native/pear-end): run /code-review (or a second-model review) and fix P1 findings before close.
7. Post a closing comment: what changed, test evidence (command + result), anything discovered worth a new bead.
acceptance_criteria: Typed PearStore/PearCore per plan API shape, green on fake day-one incl. error paths; real-device replicate test passes (A appends, B reads over swarm); deps license-checked; storage err.codes registered.
notes:
IMPLEMENTATION: PearStore/PearCore (packages/flutter_pear/lib/src/store.dart, new) -- PearStore.get({name, key}) opens/attaches a Hypercore; PearCore.append/get/replicate/close, plus a length getter + updates broadcast stream (matches PearSwarm.currentState/state's established shape). Pear.store getter wired in pear.dart. Schema additions (schema.dart + pear-end/schema.js, mirrored): PearMethod.storeGet/coreAppend/coreGet/coreReplicate/coreClose, PearEventName.coreUpdate, PearErrorCode.indexOutOfRange/coreClosed/unknownCore (all registered in the storage category). pear-end/index.js: a module-level Corestore rooted at Bare.argv[0] (same storage-path rationale as E4.4's bulk seam), a cores/closedCores registry, and per-peer replicationStreams chaining (Hypercore's replicate() must chain through a prior call's returned stream, not the raw connection twice, so a future second core -- e.g. E5.3's Hyperbee -- can share a connection safely). corestore ^7.11.0 added to pear-end/package.json; license-checked (all 82 resolved node_modules packages scanned programmatically: MIT/Apache-2.0/BSD/ISC only, matching LICENSING.md's existing corestore/hypercore footnote). flutter_pear_test's FakeBareWorklet extended to conform: store/core RPC handling, an in-memory _FakeCore data object, single-writer enforcement (only a name:-derived open can append), and a two-sided replicate handshake (merge only completes once BOTH peers have called replicate() for a (key,peer) pair, matching real Hypercore).

TESTS: packages/flutter_pear/test/store_test.dart (new, 15 tests): open-by-name, idempotent naming, append/get happy path, INDEX_OUT_OF_RANGE and CORE_CLOSED error paths (both named in this ticket's VALIDATION), updates/watch semantics, reopen-after-close, per-worklet name-key non-collision, writer-only-append enforcement, one-sided-replicate-never-syncs, and a full two-peer replicate flow (A appends before replicating, B attaches by key, both replicate, B reads A's data and later sees a live update for a post-replicate append). schema_test.dart extended for the 5 new methods/1 event/3 error codes (both the Dart-side and the JS-mirror regex checks). melos run analyze: 0 warnings across all 4 packages. melos run test --no-select: 63/63 in flutter_pear (was 59), 13/13 in flutter_pear_test, 6/6 in flutter_pear_bare -- all green. `dart run flutter_pear:pack` rebuilt the real bundle successfully with corestore's ~22 new packages linked (bare-pack --preset android), proving the dependency actually bundles/links for Android, not just unit-tests clean.

REVIEW: non-trivial diff (schema + pear-end/index.js + new wrapper), so ran 3 parallel independent review agents (JS/Dart correctness, fake-worklet conformance, schema/conventions consistency) per this ticket's BEFORE CLOSING step 6. All 3 returned substantive findings, not rubber-stamps:
- CRITICAL (JS/Dart correctness, verified against the real corestore package): STORE_GET's reopen-after-close guard only re-registered a session when `!cores.has(keyHex)` -- since `cores` is never pruned on close, reopening the SAME key after CORE_CLOSE hit the guard's false branch, so closedCores.delete() never ran and the stale, permanently-closed session stayed registered forever. Fixed: the guard also re-registers whenever `closedCores.has(keyHex)` is true. Regression test added (store_test.dart).
- MEDIUM (same agent): none of the 5 new handlers wrapped genuine Corestore/Hypercore I/O failures the way BULK_WRITE_FILE already does, so a real storage failure would surface as an uncategorized base PearException instead of the PearStorageException schema.dart's own doc comment promises. Fixed: a withStorageErrors(fn) wrapper stamps STORAGE_UNAVAILABLE onto any caught error that doesn't already have a `.code` (every error this file throws intentionally already has one, so nothing already-coded gets overwritten).
- MEDIUM (same agent): PearStore.get()'s exactly-one-of-name/key precondition was only a Dart `assert` (stripped in release builds); an unfiltered neither-given call reached real corestore and threw an opaque, uncoded "Could not derive discovery from input". Fixed: STORE_GET now validates this itself and throws a clean STORAGE_UNAVAILABLE-coded error regardless of build mode -- mirrored in the fake for conformance.
- LOW (same agent, accepted as documented tradeoff, not fixed): CORE_CLOSE marks closed before awaiting core.close(), so a call already past its open-check when close begins races teardown and gets an uncoded-then-STORAGE_UNAVAILABLE error rather than the more precise CORE_CLOSED. Deemed acceptable for a first substrate wrapper rather than adding per-core operation locking.
- CRITICAL (fake-conformance agent): the replicate() merge picked "more blocks wins" with zero validation, silently discarding the losing side's real data (and firing no event at all on a length tie) if two peers had ever appended independently before replicating. Root-caused to a deeper gap: the fake let ANY worklet append to ANY core regardless of ownership, whereas real Hypercore only allows the corestore that originally derived a core (via store.get(name:)) to write it -- a key:-attached remote core is read-only. Fixed at the root: _FakeCore now tracks a `writer` (only a name:-derived open claims it), coreAppend throws STORAGE_UNAVAILABLE from a non-owning worklet, and the merge step additionally throws a loud StateError (not a silent discard) if it ever sees two non-prefix core views anyway, as a backstop.
- HIGH (same agent): store.get(name:)'s key derivation had no per-worklet salt, so two DIFFERENT FakeBareWorklet instances calling store.get(name: sameName) collided onto the identical key -- a real conformance violation (real Corestore derives name-based keys from that corestore's own per-device primary key) and the direct enabler of the finding above. Fixed: the fake now salts with the worklet's own peerKey.
- MEDIUM (same agent): the fake fully merged and synced the moment ONE side called coreReplicate; real Hypercore requires both ends to attach their own protocol instance before any data crosses over, so a test forgetting one side's call would falsely pass against the fake and hang against the real worklet. Fixed: a per-(key,peer) replicate-offered handshake now gates the merge on both sides having called it.
- MINOR (schema/conventions agent): PearErrorCode.unknownCore's dartdoc didn't mention coreClose also throws it. Fixed (one line).
All 4 new regression tests (reopen, name-key non-collision, writer-only-append, one-sided-replicate) map directly to these fixes. Re-ran melos analyze + melos run test --no-select after every fix -- green throughout, final state above.

DEFERRED (per this project's standing "automated tests first, hardware last" decision -- same treatment as E1.5): this ticket's VALIDATION also calls for "ONE real-device integration test: append on device A, replicate over swarm, read on device B." That leg is explicitly physical two-device hardware, so it's deferred and tracked centrally in flutter_pear-doi (new ticket, filed this session) alongside E1.5 and E5.1's physical-device benchmark leg -- NOT silently dropped. Everything else in this ticket's ACCEPTANCE (typed API per plan shape, green fake-driven suite incl. error paths, deps license-checked, storage err.codes registered) is fully met now.

DISCOVERED, tracked separately: flutter_pear-pcg's scope extended (bd update) to note E5.2's JS handlers have the same "no JS-level unit test" gap as E4.4's join/leave logic -- worth noting the critical reopen bug above was caught by a human/agent line-by-line read, not any automated test (fake or real), which is exactly the kind of miss a real JS harness would catch mechanically.
close_reason: E5.2 done: PearStore/PearCore (Corestore/Hypercore wrapper 1 of 5) implemented end-to-end -- typed Dart API, pear-end JS handlers, schema entries, and a conforming fake, with 15 new tests (63/63 green in flutter_pear) covering append/get/length/watch happy path plus the INDEX_OUT_OF_RANGE and CORE_CLOSED error paths this ticket's VALIDATION named. 3 parallel independent reviews surfaced and got fixed: 1 critical real bug (a reopened-after-close core was permanently locked out), 1 critical + 2 supporting fake-conformance gaps (silent data-loss on merge, cross-worklet key collision, one-sided replicate falsely succeeding) rooted in a missing single-writer invariant now enforced, plus consistent STORAGE_UNAVAILABLE error-wrapping. Real bundle rebuilt via dart run flutter_pear:pack with corestore linked for Android, proving it actually bundles, not just unit-tests clean. Deps license-checked (MIT/Apache/BSD/ISC only). The two-device physical-replicate leg of VALIDATION is deferred with E1.5/E5.1 under new tracking ticket flutter_pear-doi, per this project's automated-first/hardware-last decision -- not silently dropped.

title: E5.1: Platform-channel throughput benchmark + bulk-transport decision record

3. Self-review the diff line-by-line against ACCEPTANCE below; every criterion demonstrably met, none "mostly".

acceptance_criteria: Reproducible harness measures channel vs file-path across 1KB-16MB on device+emulator; committed decision record picks the Hyperdrive path with numbers; no streaming built without this justification.
notes:
IMPLEMENTATION: packages/flutter_pear_example/integration_test/bulk_transport_benchmark_test.dart (new) -- a real on-device/emulator harness measuring round-trip throughput for 1KB/64KB/1MB/16MB payloads via both candidate transports: in-channel (new debug-only PearMethod.debugEcho -- a raw JSON RPC echo with no other JS-side work, added specifically so the benchmark isolates channel cost from any application-level processing) and file-path (existing PearMethod.bulkWriteFile, E4.4). The harness constructs a SECOND PearRpc sharing Pear.start()'s own worklet (verified safe by review: BareWorklet.incoming/onCrash are genuine broadcast streams, PearRpc matches responses by its own per-instance request id so a foreign response is a silent no-op, and PearRpc.dispose() never touches the shared controllers).

RESULTS (packages/flutter_pear/BENCHMARK.md, full table + analysis): 3 runs on an Android emulator, reproducible. 1KB/64KB are noise-dominated/comparable; 1MB and 16MB show file-path consistently ~2-2.5x faster (16MB: ~1.0s file-path vs ~2.6s in-channel, every run). DECISION: LOCKED default (file-path) CONFIRMED -- the numbers actively support it, not just fail to contradict it. PearDrive (E5.5) should default file transfers through a bulk.writeFile-style seam.

TESTS/VALIDATION: melos run analyze: 0 warnings across all 4 packages. melos run test --no-select: 84/84 flutter_pear, 13/13 flutter_pear_test, 6/6 flutter_pear_bare -- unaffected, all green. schema_test.dart extended for PearMethod.debugEcho (both the Dart constant and the JS-mirror check). The benchmark itself ran 3 times on a real Android emulator (emulator-5554) -- all three passed, all three showed the same qualitative pattern. Real bundle rebuilt via dart run flutter_pear:pack after adding the debug.echo handler.

REVIEW: touches pear-end/schema.dart (a new debug-only method) but the change itself is small and low-risk (a stateless identity echo, no lifecycle/data-structure complexity) -- ran one focused review agent rather than a full multi-angle workflow, proportionate to the risk. Found one real, worth-fixing issue: the benchmark's own assertions only checked payload LENGTH, not byte-for-byte content, which wouldn't have caught a transport corrupting bytes while preserving size (the exact historical bug class E4.4's frame-coalescing issue was). Fixed: both benchmark helpers now assert full byte-for-byte equality against the original random payload. Confirmed safe: debug.echo's malformed-input behavior (falls through to the same uncoded-err convention every other handler already uses, no crash), the dual-PearRpc-per-worklet pattern (verified against the real rpc.dart/bare_worklet.dart source, not just plausible-sounding), and BENCHMARK.md's arithmetic. Re-ran the benchmark on-device after the assertion fix -- still green, same pattern (16MB: file-path ~2.6x faster).

DEFERRED (per this project's standing "automated tests first, hardware last" decision, same treatment as E1.5/E5.2/E5.3): this ticket's STEPS also call for running the benchmark "on one physical device + one emulator" -- the physical-device leg is deferred and tracked in flutter_pear-doi; the emulator leg (this closing record) is real, reproducible evidence, not a placeholder. The committed harness is directly reusable for that pass whenever it runs.
close_reason: E5.1 done: real, reproducible on-device (emulator) benchmark confirms the LOCKED file-path default for bulk transport -- file-path is ~2-2.5x faster than in-channel at 1MB/16MB, consistently across 3 runs (see packages/flutter_pear/BENCHMARK.md for the full table + PearDrive/E5.5 recommendation). Harness committed at packages/flutter_pear_example/integration_test/bulk_transport_benchmark_test.dart, reusable for the deferred physical-device pass (tracked in flutter_pear-doi). New debug.echo RPC method (debug-only, mirrors debugForceError/debugForceCrash's precedent) reviewed and found safe; one real gap in the benchmark's own assertions (length-only, not content) found and fixed. melos analyze/test green throughout (84/84 flutter_pear). E5.5 (PearDrive) is now unblocked.

notes: Review process: independent Agent-tool review (6 numbered checks) found zero P1s but one real, legitimate CONCERN in my FIRST draft of the fix -- exactly described above (Build.SUPPORTED_ABIS checks device capability, not the installed package's actual native libs, so it wouldn't catch a wrong-split-on-a-capable-device scenario despite being described that way). Fixed immediately (not deferred) by redesigning around the actual native-load failure via try/catch(LinkageError) instead of a predictive heuristic, and verified on-device via dumpsys package that the ALTERNATIVE heuristic I considered (a nativeLibraryDir file check) would have been WORSE (a guaranteed false positive under extractNativeLibs=false). Reviewer also confirmed: the guard is correctly placed after the hot-restart-reattach early-return (doesn't redundantly re-check a live worklet), the exception type is correctly caught by the existing onMethodCall try/catch and surfaces as a readable Dart PlatformException, and the suggested alternative fix (an example-app-level splits{abi{}} Gradle block) is moot since flutter_pear_example/android/ is entirely untracked/regenerated (flutter create hydration), not a place to durably encode project-wide config.
close_reason: Release app-bundle (.aab) and split-APK (--split-per-abi) builds both verified working, including a real on-device release-mode boot (R8 active) with zero crash. Found and fixed a genuine packaging gap along the way: the armeabi-v7a variant both artifact types produce by default is missing this plugin's native libraries entirely (32-bit is deliberately unsupported) -- after two rejected approaches (a library-level ndk.abiFilters, confirmed to have no effect on a consuming app's splits; a device-capability precheck, correctly flagged by review as not actually testing what's installed), landed on reacting to the real native-load failure via try/catch(LinkageError) with a clear, actionable message instead of a cryptic native-loader crash. Verified via dumpsys package that a plausible alternative (a native-library-directory file check) would have been worse -- a guaranteed false positive under the modern extractNativeLibs=false packaging mode. Documented in README.md; no new consumer-rules.pro entries needed. Two-device hardware chat validation remains deferred per standing project directive.

description:

title: E9: CI/CD + publishing — reproducible bundle, license enforcement, pub.dev v0.1 (Android)
description:
GOAL: Reproducible builds, enforced licensing, and the pub.dev release train. Publish v0.1 (ANDROID-ONLY — locked; iOS is v0.2 with its own podspec/Xcode/background milestone) once the full stack + lifecycle + license assets are wired. Single release, no preview slices (user's settled office-hours decision — do not relitigate).

WORK ITEMS:
1. T9 reproducible bundle (codex #11): pin pear-end JS deps (drop caret ranges in packages/flutter_pear/pear-end/package.json); STOP .gitignoring lockfiles — commit package-lock.json so the shipped bundle is reproducible.
2. T10 license collector enforcement (codex #12): packages/flutter_pear/bin/pack.dart currently SILENTLY SKIPS modules without license files. Make it: FAIL (not skip) on missing license; reject forbidden SPDX values (GPL/AGPL/LGPL/MPL/SSPL/unlicensed); walk nested dependency trees; include native Bare Kit notices; regenerate NOTICE without stale output. Extend packages/flutter_pear/test/pack_test.dart for each behavior.
3. T11 publishing mechanics (codex #10): flutter_pear has a PATH dependency on flutter_pear_bare (packages/flutter_pear/pubspec.yaml:14) — pub.dev requires separately published packages with hosted version constraints. Define publication order (flutter_pear_bare -> flutter_pear_test -> flutter_pear) and convert path deps at publish time. Publish-on-tag workflow.
4. Compatibility table as CI contract (codex #13 + eng): plugin <-> Bare Kit <-> Hyper* versions PLUS the tested toolchain row (Flutter/AGP/Gradle/Kotlin/JDK/NDK/minSdk) — one tested contract, checked in CI against pinned versions.
5. X10 device matrix CI: arm64 device + x86_64 emulator jobs; RELEASE-mode job (R8/app-bundle) so debug never masks release breakage (codex #9).
6. X9 TTHW enforcement (locked, DX D12): the fresh-machine CI job (create app -> add package -> build -> run) gets timestamps and FAILS if clone->running exceeds the 5-minute budget. TTHW regression = red CI forever. Plus quarterly /devex-review boomerang on the live package (calendar habit — CI can't feel device auth, camera prompts, cold caches).
7. Encryption criterion test (codex #13-eng): capture transport bytes between two peers; assert an authenticated Noise session + decryption only by the intended peer ("not plaintext" alone can pass on framing/encoding).
8. GitHub Discussions + issue templates ON at publish day (TODOS.md item; templates per project_plan.md section 6 — include flutter doctor + worklet log).

ACCEPTANCE: tag -> CI green across matrix (debug+release, device+emulator, fresh-machine under 5 min, licenses enforced, compat table verified, snippets compile) -> packages land on pub.dev in order -> flutter pub add flutter_pear on a clean machine builds and runs the chat demo. pub.dev score >= 130.

title: E7: Example app DX — QR-pairing chat, desktop CLI peer, doctor tool
description:
GOAL: The example app is the product demo, the magical-moment vehicle, and the TTHW proof. Committed target (locked, DX D3): CHAMPION tier — clone -> two-peer chat in UNDER 5 MINUTES. The magical moment: a message appears on peer B and the developer realizes there is no server anywhere.

WORK ITEMS:
1. X1 QR-pairing chat example (locked, DX D4): commit the example runners (android/ hydrated and CHECKED IN — the "hydrate once with flutter create" step is gone). Phone A taps "Start room" -> shows QR of topic/invite; phone B scans -> chat connects. One flutter run per device, zero key typing. Camera-permission denial recovery + manual short-code entry fallback (codex #4). The QR scan doubles as the blind-pairing demo once PearPairing lands; before that it may encode a topic. Multi-device flutter run ergonomics documented (-d flags, two terminals).
2. X7 desktop CLI peer (locked, DX D11): dart run flutter_pear_example:peer joins a topic from the dev's laptop as the second peer — Bare runs on desktop. Solves the one-phone developer (a big slice of the persona: emulators sit behind NAT that can break UDP hole-punching). Doubles as the scriptable second peer for CI integration tests.
3. X5 doctor tool (user decision, DX D9): dart run flutter_pear:doctor — connectivity check, DHT reachability, NAT type; names the blocker on a UDP-blocked network. --report prints a telemetry-free support bundle (plugin/native/bundle versions, ABI, Android version, sanitized recent log) for pasting into GitHub issues (codex #18). Scope note: doctor is a RUNTIME diagnostic; install-time failures (Gradle/NDK) are the error catalog + troubleshooting docs' job (codex #11).
4. Emulator/device pairing combos verified and documented: device<->device, device<->emulator, emulator<->emulator, device<->desktop-peer — README states exactly which work.
5. README GIF: 15-second capture of the QR->chat flow (passive preview that converts browsers into cloners).

ACCEPTANCE: stopwatch run on a fresh machine: clone -> melos bootstrap -> flutter run x2 -> QR scan -> message on both screens in under 5 minutes. One-phone path: phone + laptop CLI peer reaches the same moment. Doctor on a hostile network names the blocker instead of shrugging.

title: E5: Data-structure wrappers — Corestore/Hypercore → Hyperbee → Hyperdrive → pairing → Autobase-last
description:
GOAL: Fan out the typed Dart wrappers for the full Pear stack against the locked schema, in DEPENDENCY ORDER (locked — the "any order" premise was wrong): Corestore + Hypercore are the substrate everything sits on and come FIRST; then Hyperbee; then Hyperdrive (streaming risk); then blind-pairing; AUTOBASE LAST (multi-writer risk).

METHOD (locked): hand-write the first two wrappers (PearStore/PearCore against Corestore/Hypercore, then PearBee) directly against the schema, green on the flutter_pear_test fake the day they're written. Decide codegen ONLY if the third wrapper's boilerplate is provably mechanical and repetitive. Every wrapper: expose the JS module over RPC in pear-end, add the typed Dart wrapper — never reimplement any protocol in Dart.

PER-WRAPPER CHECKLIST (applies to every package below):
- pear-end: expose the module over the schema (request/response + events, typed err.codes registered in the error catalog)
- Dart: typed wrapper — Futures for calls, broadcast Streams for events, Uint8List for binary, PearKey for keys, dispose() per Flutter norms, dartdoc on every public symbol
- fake: extend flutter_pear_test in lockstep
- tests: unit tests against the fake (happy + error paths) AND at least one real-device integration test against the real worklet (guards fake/real drift — codex #9)
- licensing: before adding ANY pear-end dependency confirm MIT/Apache-2.0/BSD/ISC; reject GPL/AGPL/LGPL/MPL/SSPL/unlicensed; regenerate THIRD_PARTY_LICENSES via dart run flutter_pear:pack

ORDER + RISK NOTES:
1. PearStore/PearCore (Corestore/Hypercore): substrate. Storage paths per platform. err.codes -> PearStorageException.
2. PearBee (Hyperbee): get/put/del, range streams, watch. Range streams = first real use of event-stream subscribe/unsubscribe.
3. PearDrive (Hyperdrive): BULK RISK. Locked transport decision: file-path import/export (worklet writes to disk, Dart reads the file) or pull-based bounded reads — NOT in-channel streaming unless the throughput benchmark demands it. A bulk transfer large enough to require chunking must complete without exhausting memory.
4. PearPairing (blind-pairing): invites, device linking. Feeds the example app's QR flow.
5. PearBase (Autobase) LAST — HARD. Locked decision (codex #2): do NOT expose generic open/apply (incompatible with zero-JS; Dart callbacks across RPC during deterministic indexing are slow/reentrant/failure-prone). Ship PREBUILT MERGE RECIPES in the pear-end (last-writer-wins, ordered-log, CRDT-map); Dart selects by name. Generic apply = documented custom-worklet advanced path only.

PINNED OPEN QUESTIONS (resolve during, not before): reconnect/delivery semantics after network switch (M1); topic/key persistence, backup & device migration (M2).

Reference: ~/.gstack/projects/andrewloable-flutter_pear/andrewloable-main-design-20260703-131146.md (P2 caveat, Next Steps 3b/4, T12) and project_plan.md section 4 (API coverage map)
acceptance_criteria: Each wrapper: typed Dart API green on the fake day-one + >=1 real-device integration test; Hyperdrive bulk via file-path import/export completes a chunk-scale transfer without exhausting memory; Autobase ships prebuilt recipes (LWW/ordered-log/CRDT-map) selectable by name, NO generic apply in the default path; all pear-end deps license-checked.
notes:
E5.4b CODEGEN RE-EVALUATION (after wrapper 3=PearDrive/Hyperdrive closed, per E5.4's own deferred checkpoint): NO-GO, and this time conclusively -- not "revisit later."

QUANTIFIED EVIDENCE WITH 3 SAMPLES (vs E5.4's 2): store.dart 119 lines, bee.dart 202, drive.dart 121 (442 total). Tests: store_test 268, bee_test 342, drive_test 266 (876 total). pear-end handler-case sections: E5.2 ~80 lines, E5.3 ~124, E5.5 ~146 (growing, not shrinking, as each wrapper's real underlying JS library -- Hypercore's log, Hyperbee's tree, Hyperdrive's dual-core file/blob split -- adds its own genuinely novel translation work). Mechanical fraction still measures ~35-40% (open validation, reopen-after-close guard, error wrapping, replicate/close idempotency, fake writer+merge+replicate-handshake, schema doc shape, several near-identical test cases) -- consistent with E5.4's 2-sample estimate, now confirmed with a 3rd, architecturally distinct wrapper (file/blob storage, not another log or tree).

NEW, DECISIVE FINDING this 3rd sample surfaces: the "mechanical" reopen-after-close pattern is NOT actually template-safe even within its own established shape -- E5.5's review caught DRIVE_OPEN getting a SUBTLY WORSE version of a bug E5.2's STORE_GET already had (a discarded session leaking on redundant reopen; E5.5's version compounded it by also constructing a full second Hyperdrive with 2 more sessions before the registration guard). A follow-up (flutter_pear-0md) now tracks retrofitting E5.2/E5.3 for the SAME lesson E5.5 only learned by being reviewed. This means even the "boilerplate" 35-40% has real, non-obvious correctness subtlety that a naive template would not have gotten right without the SAME review rigor a generator would supposedly save -- three independent hand-written attempts at this "mechanical" pattern produced three different bugs.

THE DECISIVE REASON, though, is upstream of all that: **the remaining two wrappers in this epic's own locked roadmap do not share wrappers 1-3's shape at all.**
- E5.6 (PearPairing, wrapper 4): create/accept/revoke invite -- a handshake/protocol flow with no open-by-name/key, no get/put, no writer/reader concept, no replicate() in the same sense. Nothing like wrappers 1-3's template.
- E5.7 (Autobase, wrapper 5): LOCKED (codex #2) to explicitly NOT expose generic open/apply -- ships prebuilt merge recipes selected by name instead, precisely because wrappers 1-3's flexible single-writer pattern doesn't fit Autobase's multi-writer semantics.

A generator scoped to wrappers 1-3's proven pattern (per the codex audit note's own plan, "the generator, if built, applies from wrapper 4 onward") would have ZERO remaining consumers within this project's actual roadmap -- E5.6 and E5.7 are both confirmed, by their own ticket descriptions, not to fit it. Building one now would be pure speculative investment with no earned repetition ahead of it, which is exactly what this epic's own governing principle forbids ("codegen is EARNED by felt repetition, never a speculative investment").

DECISION: NO-GO, final for this epic's roadmap. No further "re-evaluate after wrapper N" follow-up filed -- there is no wrapper 6 planned, and if a genuinely new corestore-backed, single-writer, replicate-able data structure is ever added to a FUTURE version of this plan, that would be a fresh decision with its own fresh checkpoint, not a resumption of this one.

Full decision also recorded in ~/.gstack/projects/andrewloable-flutter_pear/decisions.jsonl.

title: E6: Lifecycle + hot-restart correctness
description:
GOAL: Lifecycle and hot-restart correctness — constraints with their own success criteria (locked: "schedule them, don't assume they fall out for free"). This is the first thing every dev hits; it ships in v0.1.

WORK ITEMS:
1. Auto suspend/resume wired to AppLifecycleState with sane linger, overridable (opt-out + manual suspend()/resume() stay public on BareWorklet — packages/flutter_pear_bare/lib/src/bare_worklet.dart already has the state machine; this epic wires the Flutter lifecycle observer + linger timer and pipes real suspend/resume through the native side, which currently no-ops in FlutterPearBarePlugin.kt onMethodCall "suspend"/"resume")
2. Hot restart determinism (locked rule): on attach, compare session nonce + bundle version (contract-spine handshake): reattach when the existing worklet is healthy AND version-matched, otherwise cleanly kill and restart. Never require an app reinstall. The native `started` boolean alone loses authority when the engine detaches (codex #6) — worklet identity must live with the worklet, not the plugin instance.
3. Background reality documentation (codex #8 fold): document loudly what Android actually allows (Doze, OEM task killers, foreground-service option) so an OS-killed swarm isn't blamed on the library. Foreground is the supported guarantee; background is best-effort per OS. Surface suspension state via the X8 connection-state stream so end-user apps can render it.

ACCEPTANCE (from plan success criteria):
- Backgrounding suspends the swarm; foregrounding resumes it; linger window honored; override works
- Hot restart with unchanged bundle -> reattach, session continues, no duplicate worklet
- Hot restart with CHANGED pear-end bundle -> kill + clean restart, no stale-worklet conversation
- Doze/OEM-kill behavior documented per-OS with the foreground-service escape hatch

Reference: ~/.gstack/projects/andrewloable-flutter_pear/andrewloable-main-design-20260703-131146.md (Constraints, Next Steps step 5, Success Criteria) and project_plan.md section 6
acceptance_criteria: Background suspends / foreground resumes with linger + override; hot restart: version-matched healthy worklet reattaches, changed bundle kills+restarts cleanly, never reinstall; Doze/OEM-kill reality documented with foreground-service escape hatch; suspension visible on the state stream.

title: E3: flutter_pear_test — in-memory fake swarm/core (schema conformance consumer)
description:
GOAL: Build the flutter_pear_test package — an in-memory fake swarm/core that satisfies the RPC schema so (a) all Dart-wrapper work proceeds in parallel with the risky native track, and (b) app devs can unit-test P2P logic without radios or peers. This is a shipped, first-class package (already in the planned monorepo layout), not throwaway test scaffolding.

AUTHORITY RULE (locked): the fake is a CONFORMANCE CONSUMER of the schema. It never defines the contract. If fake and schema disagree, the schema wins and the fake is fixed.

HONESTY RULE (locked, codex #9): the fake is NOT a conformance test of the real system. Matching RPC envelopes says nothing about Hypercore storage semantics, replication, Autobase ordering, swarm races, or crash behavior. Every data-structure package must ALSO ship at least one real-device integration test against the real worklet (those tests live in each wrapper's epic; this epic provides the fake only).

WORK ITEMS:
1. New package packages/flutter_pear_test with its own pubspec, LICENSE (MIT), CHANGELOG, README (pub.dev score rules apply here too — doc every public symbol)
2. In-memory fake implementing the schema surface used by PearSwarm/PearConnection first: join/leave, connection events, data frames, close; deterministic and instant (no real timers where avoidable)
3. Fake failure injection: expose test hooks to simulate worklet crash, call timeout, stale-nonce frame, connection drop, UDP-blocked network (drives the X8 state machine to 'failed') — these exist so the 7 spine failure-path tests and app-dev tests can exercise error handling
4. Grow the fake surface in lockstep as each wrapper epic lands (fake Corestore/Hypercore/Hyperbee/Hyperdrive/pairing/Autobase recipes)
5. Wire into melos: bootstrap, analyze, test all pass from repo root

ACCEPTANCE: an app dev can write a unit test exercising two fake peers exchanging messages, plus every error path, with zero platform channels, zero radios, zero real peers. flutter_pear's own spine tests run against this fake in CI on every push.

Reference: ~/.gstack/projects/andrewloable-flutter_pear/andrewloable-main-design-20260703-131146.md (Recommended Approach fold-in from C, Next Steps step 2)
acceptance_criteria: App dev can unit-test two fake peers exchanging messages plus every error path with zero radios/peers; failure-injection hooks drive all spine error tests; fake never contradicts schema (schema wins on disagreement).
notes: All 4 children closed. FakeBareWorklet (E3.2) is a genuine conformance consumer of the RPC schema -- PearSwarm/PearConnection run completely unmodified against it, proven by a dedicated cross-package test. All 6 failure-injection hooks (E3.3) exist and each drives a green test proving the app-visible outcome via REAL production PearRpc/PearSwarm/PearConnection, with fake-driven variants covering all of E2.2 through E2.7 living directly in flutter_pear's own spine test files. A CI job (E3.4) now runs the full stack on every push with zero devices, and along the way found + fixed a real melos tooling bug (interactive prompt under packageFilters) that would have made the CI job (and local dev) unreliable. A forward-looking PearSwarm API question (a synchronous connections snapshot, mirroring E2.7's currentState) surfaced while building the fake is tracked separately in flutter_pear-4w6, correctly out of this epic's scope.
close_reason: E3: flutter_pear_test complete. App devs can unit-test two fake peers exchanging messages plus every RPC-spine error path with zero radios/peers/devices -- proven end-to-end via cross-package conformance tests, all 6 failure-injection hooks, and a new CI job running the whole Dart suite on every push. The fake never contradicted the schema; where a design question surfaced (a possible PearSwarm.connections API improvement), it was tracked separately rather than resolved unilaterally inside this epic.

title: Harden E5.2/E5.3's STORE_GET/BEE_OPEN: closed session leaked on a redundant reopen while already registered
description:
CONTEXT: found during E5.5's (flutter_pear-2vz.5) independent review, which caught the SAME pattern in DRIVE_OPEN and fixed it there (see that ticket's closing notes). The reviewer explicitly noted STORE_GET (pear-end/index.js) and BEE_OPEN share a lesser version of the same leak: `core = store.get({name: p.name})` (or `store.get(Buffer.from(p.key,'hex'))`) always constructs a FRESH Hypercore session object, every single call -- if the resulting key is already registered in `cores`/`bees` and not closed, that freshly-fetched session is silently discarded with no `.close()` call, leaking one Hypercore session per redundant re-open call for the life of the worklet.

Concrete trigger: an app calling `pear.store.get(name: 'x')` (or `pear.bee(name: 'x')`) more than once while the first handle is still open (a legitimate, documented-as-idempotent usage pattern -- "get() by name is idempotent" is literally one of this repo's own test names) leaks one Hypercore session per extra call.

Unlike DRIVE_OPEN's version of this bug (fixed in E5.5 by deferring the heavier Hyperdrive construction inside the registration guard), STORE_GET/BEE_OPEN's OWN core-fetch is unavoidably the first thing that has to happen (there's no separate "scratch" object to defer) -- so the fix here is different: either (a) close the freshly-fetched core/bee's underlying core when discarding it on the already-registered path, or (b) check the registry BEFORE calling store.get() at all if there's a way to compute the deterministic key without opening a session (verify whether corestore exposes a synchronous name->key derivation that doesn't require a full session).

STEPS: read pear-end/index.js's current STORE_GET and BEE_OPEN cases; read node_modules/corestore for session/refcounting behavior to pick the safest fix; apply it to both; add a regression test (open the same name twice while the first is still open, e.g. via a fake-level introspection hook analogous to activeBeeWatchCount, or a JS-level session-count assertion if flutter_pear-pcg's JS test harness exists by then).

title: Harden worklet-generation handoff against stale platform-channel messages
description: CONTEXT: found during E2.6 (flutter_pear-7be.6) review. flutter_pear_bare's _ipc and _control (bare_worklet.dart) are STATIC MethodChannel/BasicMessageChannel fields shared across all BareWorklet Dart instances; each generation transition (Pear.start's version-mismatch kill+restart, or a hot-restart reattach) reassigns the handler via setMessageHandler/setMethodCallHandler rather than using a per-generation channel. Flutter's engine buffers a channel message that arrives with no handler currently registered, replaying it to whichever handler is registered next -- so a stale worklet generation's message (e.g. a delayed workletCrash self-report, or in principle any IPC data frame) that is still in flight when Dart swaps in a new BareWorklet's handler can be delivered to the NEW generation's handler instead of being dropped. PearRpc._onFrame's narrow pre-session workletCrash exemption (added in E2.6) knowingly accepts this residual risk rather than closing it -- see its doc comment in rpc.dart. The identical risk class already exists, unaddressed, in the native onCrash backstop's control channel (bare_worklet.dart's _onControlCall / FlutterPearBarePlugin.kt's reportUnexpectedExit), independent of anything E2.6 added.
acceptance_criteria: Either (a) a documented argument for why this residual risk is acceptable long-term (not just as an E2.6-scoped trade-off), or (b) a generation-tagging or channel-draining mechanism (e.g. stamp/verify a generation id at the platform-channel layer itself, not just in the JSON envelope) that closes the gap for both the IPC data channel and the control channel. Add a regression test that proves a stale generation's message can no longer be misattributed to a new one.
notes: Not blocking E2.6's close -- the failure mode (a false WORKLET_CRASHED report) is loud and recoverable, not silent or corrupting, and requires two independent low-probability events (a real crash of the old worklet racing an unrelated restart). Deferred as a deliberate scope cut, not an oversight.

title: Bare Kit worklet fails to boot on BYD head unit: dlopen libnativehelper.so not found
description:
libbare-kit.so (bare-kit v2.3.0's own prebuilt binary, the latest release as of 2026-07-03 per upstream's releases page) directly declares NEEDED libnativehelper.so (confirmed via llvm-readelf -d against the extracted APK's lib/arm64-v8a/libbare-kit.so). libnativehelper.so is a private Android platform library; since API 24 the dynamic linker blocks third-party apps from resolving non-NDK platform-private libraries via any path (System.loadLibrary, DT_NEEDED, or direct dlopen). On the BYD AUTO head unit (Android 10 / API 29, automotive OEM ROM) this hard-fails Worklet.start() with PlatformException(worklet_start_failed, 'dlopen failed: library "libnativehelper.so" not found'). Reproduced on 2026-07-03 once flutter_pear-dpl's storage blocker was cleared and a real install was finally possible.

Critically: the SAME app (same debug APK, same libbare-kit.so) boots cleanly with NO error on a stock Android Studio emulator (Medium_Phone_API_35, Google APIs, API 35) -- logcat shows 'Load .../libbare-kit.so using ns clns-7 from class loader ... ok' and the app reaches 'Fully drawn' with no exception. This localizes the failure to BYD's specific ROM/linker-namespace configuration (its automotive AOSP build likely has a more restrictive public-library allowlist than a stock Google image), not to bare-kit itself or to our Gradle wiring -- the identical bytes load fine under a standard Android linker config.

Per developer decision (2026-07-03): use the Android emulator as the primary dev/test target going forward; only re-test against the physical BYD unit once things are stable there. The flutter_pear_example app has been uninstalled from the BYD device for now.
notes: Diagnostic commands used: 'llvm-readelf -d libbare-kit.so | grep NEEDED' from NDK 28.2.13676358's prebuilt toolchain against the extracted debug APK. bare-kit releases checked via GitHub (v2.3.0 is latest as of 2026-07-03; no newer release exists to bump to). Related: bare-kit's own AndroidManifest declares minSdkVersion 31, two above BYD's API 29 -- possibly related to why BYD's older/more customized linker config differs from a modern stock image, but not confirmed as the root cause; a real API 31+ physical device would help isolate API-level vs ROM-specific causation.
close_reason: Developer decision (2026-07-03): BYD AUTO head unit is removed from flutter_pear's supported/target device pool entirely -- not being pursued as a bug to fix. Root cause (BYD's automotive ROM linker namespace blocking libbare-kit.so's libnativehelper.so dependency) stays recorded above for reference, but no further investigation into BYD's ROM/linker config is planned. Local development and validation now targets the Android emulator (Medium_Phone_API_35 et al., already installed) as primary; the flutter_pear_example app was uninstalled from the BYD unit. Any future physical-device testing (E1.5 two-device gate, on-device checks) should use a different physical Android device, not BYD.

title: On-device validation blocked: BYD test unit has no free storage
description:
The only physical Android device available in this environment (a BYD AUTO head unit, arm64-v8a, Android 10/API 29, connected via network adb at 192.168.0.251:5555) has only ~2GB free out of 55GB (97% used, ~40GB of which is BladeWatch dashcam video). Even an 82.5MB release-mode flutter_pear_example APK fails to install with "Requested internal only, but not enough space". Tried: installLocation=auto manifest attribute (no effect — adb install still requests internal-only), adb install -s (forcing SD-card install; not supported on this device/Android version — "Unknown option -s"). Did not attempt to free space by deleting BladeWatch footage or other data without explicit developer permission.

This blocks the on-device portions of E1.2 (app installs and launches), E1.3 (bytes flow Dart<->worklet on-device), and E1.4 (forced-error frame produces a typed Dart exception on-device) validation steps. Per developer decision (2026-07-03), these tasks proceed with build-only / mocked-channel / unit-level validation, with the actual on-device confirmation deferred to this issue.

WORK: developer frees space on the BYD unit (or supplies another arm64 Android device), then someone re-runs the on-device checks for E1.2/E1.3/E1.4's worklet boot + IPC round trip + forced-error path, ideally before/alongside E1.5's two-device gate proof (which also needs hardware the developer must supply).
acceptance_criteria: BYD unit (or a substitute arm64 device) has enough free storage to install a debug flutter_pear_example APK, OR a different physical device is supplied; on-device checks from E1.2/E1.3/E1.4 are re-run and confirmed passing on real hardware.
close_reason: Storage blocker resolved: developer freed BYD device storage (~2GB -> 40GB free, confirmed 2026-07-03). Re-ran on-device install: app installs and launches successfully on BYD (storage issue is gone), BUT hit a new, distinct, real bug -- Worklet.start() fails with a dlopen error for libnativehelper.so, specific to BYD's ROM (does not reproduce on an Android emulator). Tracked separately as flutter_pear-1y1 since it's a different root cause than what this issue was scoped to. Per developer decision (2026-07-03): use an Android emulator (Medium_Phone_API_35, already installed locally) as the primary dev/test target going forward; flutter_pear_example has been uninstalled from the BYD unit; only return to BYD hardware once things are stable on emulator.

title: E8.8: Reconcile project_plan.md (+CLAUDE.md status) with the approved design decisions
description:
CONTEXT: E8 docs. Gap found in plan-vs-beads audit (2026-07-03): the design doc's codex #14 finding requires "Reconcile the design doc WITH project_plan.md — do not let them drift", and CLAUDE.md declares project_plan.md the authoritative spec that must be kept in sync when scope changes. 2. Sections 4+5 stage releases (Corestore 0.2, Hyperbee/Hyperdrive 0.3, Autobase/pairing 0.4) — LOCKED: single v0.1 release with the full stack, no preview slices. Update the coverage-map version column + milestones to the epic structure (E1-E9).
3. Section 6 "Time-to-first-success < 15 minutes" and section 8 "<15 min" — LOCKED (DX D3/D12): champion tier, clone -> two-peer chat < 5 minutes, CI-timed + quarterly human audit.
4. Section 6 "starter repo" — moved post-v0.1, build only on observed friction (bead flutter_pear-uqs).

title: E9.6: Noise confidentiality proof — capture-based, with negative control
description:
CONTEXT: E9 (codex #13-eng). The encryption success criterion must be REAL: "payload is not plaintext" can pass on framing/encoding alone. Prove an authenticated Noise session with confidentiality.
FILES: integration test (device or desktop-peer based, packages/flutter_pear_example or a test pkg), possibly a packet-capture harness (tcpdump on the desktop peer side is simplest — capture on the LAPTOP end of a phone<->laptop session from E7.3).
STEPS:
1. Establish a phone<->desktop-peer session; capture transport bytes on the desktop side while sending a known high-entropy-marker plaintext.
2. Assert: the marker does NOT appear in captured bytes; session stats from the worklet confirm a completed Noise handshake with the expected remote key (authenticated, not just encrypted).
3. Negative control: same capture on a raw unencrypted local socket DOES find the marker (proves the harness can see plaintext).
VALIDATION: test runs documented + repeatable (may be semi-manual with the runbook pattern); both assertions + the negative control pass; results posted on the epic.

acceptance_criteria: Captured transport bytes never contain the plaintext marker while a raw-socket control does; worklet confirms authenticated handshake with expected key; a wrong-keypair peer fails the handshake and cannot decrypt captures; repeatable runbook + results on the epic.
notes: CODEX AUDIT FIX: add the third assertion — wrong-peer rejection: a third peer with a different keypair attempting to join/decrypt the session is REJECTED (cannot complete the handshake) and captures from the legitimate session are not decryptable with the third peer's keys. 'Intended peer only' must be tested, not inferred from ciphertext opacity.

Therefore: this checkpoint may conclude GO-pending — in which case PearDrive (E5.5) is STILL written by hand and the generator, if built, applies from wrapper 4 (pairing) onward only. A GO here never causes wrapper 3 to be generated.
close_reason: E5.4 decision: NO-GO for codegen right now. Diff-reviewed store.dart vs bee.dart + their pear-end/fake/test counterparts (line counts and structural comparison posted in full on epic E5's notes). Mechanical fraction estimated ~35-40% (open validation, reopen-after-close guard, error wrapping, replicate/close idempotency, fake writer+merge+handshake, schema shape, several near-identical test cases) -- real but not dominant, and critically: 100% of the 3 real bugs E5.3's review caught lived in the semantic/bespoke fraction (the range/watch subscribe-lifecycle, the actual data-model translation) a generator would never touch. Only 2 samples exist and the original locked decision's own threshold is 'by the third wrapper' -- this checkpoint intentionally ran one wrapper early per its own design. No generator built (per VALIDATION). Decision recorded both as a bd comment on flutter_pear-2vz and in ~/.gstack/projects/andrewloable-flutter_pear/decisions.jsonl. Filed flutter_pear-y3g (blocked on E5.5) to redo this with 3 real samples once Hyperdrive's genuinely different file-tree API lands. No code changed by this task -- melos analyze still green across all 4 packages.

title: E5.4b: Re-evaluate codegen decision after wrapper 3 (E5.5 Hyperdrive) closes
description:
CONTEXT: E5.4 (flutter_pear-2vz.4) checked in after only 2 hand-written wrappers (PearStore/PearCore, PearBee) and concluded NO-GO for now -- see its closing notes and the epic (flutter_pear-2vz)'s bd comment for the full quantified evidence. The ORIGINAL locked decision (project plan) says codegen is only earned "by the THIRD wrapper" if boilerplate is provably mechanical -- E5.4 ran one wrapper early as a checkpoint, not the real decision point.

This ticket is that real decision point: once E5.5 (PearDrive/Hyperdrive, wrapper 3) closes, redo E5.4's diff-review/quantification with THREE samples instead of two. Hyperdrive's file-tree API is expected to look meaningfully different from both the append-log (Hypercore) and ordered-KV (Hyperbee) shapes already built, which should either strengthen or weaken the "provably mechanical" case with real evidence either way.

STEPS: same as E5.4's (diff-review the 3 wrappers + their pear-end/fake/test counterparts, quantify mechanical vs semantic fraction, post a decision as a bd comment on the epic AND a gstack decision log entry, GO spawns a scope-fenced generator bead pinned to exactly the measured mechanical fraction).
close_reason: E5.4b decision: NO-GO, and conclusively this time. 3-sample diff-review (store.dart/bee.dart/drive.dart + their pear-end/fake/test counterparts, full quantification on epic E5's notes) confirms E5.4's ~35-40% mechanical-fraction estimate, but the decisive finding is different: E5.6 (PearPairing) is an invite/handshake protocol with no open-by-name/key, get/put, writer, or replicate() shape at all, and E5.7 (Autobase) is explicitly LOCKED to ship prebuilt merge recipes instead of generic open/apply since it's multi-writer. Neither remaining wrapper in this epic's roadmap fits the template a generator would be built from -- so a generator has zero consumers left to justify it. No generator built; no further re-evaluation ticket filed, since there's no wrapper 6 planned. Decision recorded on epic E5 and in the gstack decision log.

title: Consider a synchronous snapshot accessor for PearSwarm.connections (mirroring currentState)
description: CONTEXT: found while building E3.2's FakeBareWorklet/fake_worklet_test.dart. PearSwarm.connections is a plain broadcast Stream<PearConnection> with no replay to a late subscriber -- same class of issue PearSwarm.state had before E2.7 added PearSwarm.currentState (a synchronous getter) specifically to guarantee a caller reading it right after join() resolves never misses the first transition. connections has no equivalent: if a peer connects very quickly relative to join()'s own ack (plausible on a fast LAN, and DEFINITELY true against FakeBareWorklet, which connects an already-present peer with zero discovery delay), a caller that does anything else awaited between join() resolving and subscribing to .connections can miss that first connection permanently. Demonstrated concretely in fake_worklet_test.dart: a test inserting one extra unrelated await between two peers' joins caused exactly this race, fixed by reordering to subscribe to .connections immediately after each swarm's own join() with nothing else awaited in between -- fragile, easy to get wrong (I got it wrong once myself while writing the test), and documented as a sharp edge in FakeBareWorklet's own doc comment rather than fixed at the PearSwarm level.
acceptance_criteria: Either (a) a documented decision that this is an acceptable, sufficiently-rare edge case not worth a currentState-style fix (real Hyperswarm's DHT discovery latency makes the race window much narrower in practice than in the fake), or (b) add something like List<PearConnection> get establishedConnections (a synchronous snapshot of connections so far, mirroring currentState's role for the state stream) and update fake_worklet_test.dart / swarm_test.dart to use it where it removes fragility.
notes: Not blocking E3.2's close -- the fake itself is correct and the test suite passes; this is a forward-looking API-ergonomics question surfaced by building the fake, relevant to E5's data-structure wrappers which will also consume PearSwarm.

title: Harden E4.4: argv null-safety guard + JS-level unit tests for pear-end's dynamic join/leave
description: CONTEXT: found during E4.4 (flutter_pear-47k.4) review. Two small, non-blocking gaps: (1) pear-end/index.js's BULK_STORAGE_DIR = path.join(Bare.argv[0], pear-bulk) is computed at module top-level with no guard against Bare.argv[0] being undefined -- currently unreachable in practice since FlutterPearBarePlugin.kt always passes the app files dir via argv on every startWorklet() boot path, but a defensive check (or a clearer thrown message) would make a future regression fail loudly rather than with a generic path.join TypeError. (2) pear-end/ has zero JS-level unit tests -- the only automated coverage of E4.4's new dynamic SWARM_JOIN/SWARM_LEAVE logic (the topics Map, idempotent re-join, real swarm.join/leave calls) is indirect, via swarm.dart's pre-existing fake-worklet tests, which never exercise pear-end's actual JS at all. A plain Node-run test stubbing Hyperswarm's join/leave (and the BareKit/Bare globals) could cover handle()'s SWARM_JOIN/SWARM_LEAVE branches without a real device or network.
acceptance_criteria: (1) BULK_STORAGE_DIR computation (or its first use) throws a clear, named error if Bare.argv[0] is missing, with a regression test. (2) At least one new test file (plain node --test or similar, not requiring bare-pack/device) exercises handle()'s SWARM_JOIN/SWARM_LEAVE logic against a stubbed Hyperswarm, covering: idempotent re-join, leave-then-rejoin, and that swarm.join/leave are called with the right topic buffer.
notes: Scope also now covers E5.2 (flutter_pear-2vz.2, Corestore/Hypercore wrapper): pear-end/index.js's STORE_GET/CORE_APPEND/CORE_GET/CORE_REPLICATE/CORE_CLOSE handlers have the same gap as E4.4's SWARM_JOIN/SWARM_LEAVE -- only indirectly covered, via flutter_pear_test's FakeBareWorklet (a parallel Dart reimplementation of the same wire contract, not the real JS). A real, JS-level review during E5.2 caught a critical bug in this exact code (STORE_GET's reopen-after-close logic silently left a key permanently marked closed) that no automated test -- fake or real -- would have caught on its own; only manual line-by-line reading of index.js against the fake found it. That's a concrete argument for whatever JS test harness this ticket ends up building to cover E5.2's handlers too, not just E4.4's.

title: Recurring: quarterly /devex-review boomerang on the live package (first: ~3mo post-publish)
description:
CONTEXT: DX review decision D12 (locked): TTHW measurement = CI timer (E9.3, catches build-time regressions) + QUARTERLY human /devex-review boomerang on the LIVE package — the human audit catches what CI cannot feel: device authorization prompts, camera permissions, cold Gradle caches on real machines, docs confusion, pairing UX.
CADENCE: first run ~3 months after v0.1 publish; repeat quarterly. Each run: install from pub.dev on a machine that has never built the project, stopwatch the clone/add->two-peer-chat flow against the <5 min champion commitment, run /devex-review, file beads for regressions.
NOTE: this bead is the recurring anchor — after each run, post results as a comment and re-open/defer for the next quarter rather than closing permanently (or clone a fresh bead per run).
acceptance_criteria: Each quarterly run posts: stopwatch TTHW from a cold machine vs the <5 min commitment, /devex-review score, and beads filed for any regression; bead re-opened/deferred for the next cycle.

title: Post-v0.1: starter template repo (build only on observed friction)
description:
CONTEXT: Post-v0.1 DevEx item from project_plan.md section 6, deliberately excluded from the v0.1 epics: a `flutter create --template`-style starter repo. For v0.1 the committed example app + README quickstart cover time-to-first-success; this exists to reduce day-one friction for NEW apps (not clones) if adoption data shows people struggle to wire flutter_pear into a fresh project.
TRIGGER: revisit when post-publish issues/questions show new-project setup friction (watch GitHub issues + Discussions after v0.1). If no friction signal appears, close as not-needed rather than building speculatively.
SCOPE when triggered: a template repo (or `dart create` brick) with flutter_pear pre-wired: dependency, lifecycle setup, a minimal joined-swarm screen, doctor invocation in README.
acceptance_criteria: Either a published starter template that a new user can create-and-run in under 5 minutes, OR a documented close-as-not-needed decision citing absence of setup-friction signals.
